question: What does 'A.L.s.' stand for in a bookseller's catalogue?
answers: Autograph letter, signed
Authenticated library stamp
Autograph label, supplementary
All leaves, sound
correct: Autograph letter, signed

question: What does 'T.e.g.' mean in a book description?
answers: Top edges gilt
Text entirely genuine
Title edge gilt
Tooled edges, gilt
correct: Top edges gilt

question: What does 'w.a.f.' mean at auction?
answers: With all faults
Wrappers as found
With additional folios
Written and finished
correct: With all faults

question: What does the term 'association copy' describe?
answers: A copy connected to the author, a person linked to the author, or someone associated with its contents
A copy sold through a booksellers' association
A copy with an association bookplate
A copy that forms part of a matched set
correct: A copy connected to the author, a person linked to the author, or someone associated with its contents

question: In book collecting, what is an 'advance copy'?
answers: A copy circulated before publication to reviewers, booksellers or book-club judges
The first copy pulled from the press
A copy sold in advance of the stated publication date
A proof copy corrected by the author
correct: A copy circulated before publication to reviewers, booksellers or book-club judges

question: What does 'all published' mean in a catalogue description of a multi-volume work?
answers: The series was never completed and this is all that exists
All volumes were published simultaneously
Every volume has been published in a revised edition
The set has been published by multiple publishers
correct: The series was never completed and this is all that exists

question: What is 'Americana' in book collecting terms?
answers: Books about, connected with, or printed in America, distinct from books merely by American writers
Any book written by an American author
Books printed in the United States before 1800
Books about American history published in Britain
correct: Books about, connected with, or printed in America, distinct from books merely by American writers

question: What does 'autograph' mean when used as an adjective in book catalogues?
answers: In the hand of the author or person referenced, whether signed or not
Signed only by the author on the title page
Printed with a facsimile of the author's signature
Authenticated by a third party
correct: In the hand of the author or person referenced, whether signed or not

question: What is a 'cancel' in bibliographical terms?
answers: Any part of a book substituted for what was originally printed
A leaf intentionally left blank by the printer
A page removed for censorship that has not been replaced
A bookplate removed from a previous owner's copy
correct: Any part of a book substituted for what was originally printed

question: What is the original leaf or sheet called that a cancel replaces?
answers: The cancelland (or cancellum)
The cancelled leaf
The suppressed leaf
The replaced folio
correct: The cancelland (or cancellum)

question: What is the name for a binding style where the spine and corners are leather but the sides are cloth or paper?
answers: Half bound
Quarter bound
Three-quarter bound
Cased binding
correct: Half bound

question: What distinguishes a 'bound' book from a 'cased' book?
answers: In a bound book, the sections are sewn directly to bands whose ends are laced into the boards; in a cased book the case is made separately and the book inserted
A bound book has a leather covering; a cased book has cloth
A bound book is sewn; a cased book is glued
A bound book uses raised bands; a cased book has a hollow back
correct: In a bound book, the sections are sewn directly to bands whose ends are laced into the boards; in a cased book the case is made separately and the book inserted

question: What are 'raised bands' on the spine of a book?
answers: The external ridges on the spine created by the cords or bands on which the quires are sewn
Decorative gold strips tooled horizontally across the spine
Bands of leather raised above the boards to protect the spine
The hemp cords visible on the inside of a book's spine
correct: The external ridges on the spine created by the cords or bands on which the quires are sewn

question: What does 'blind tooling' or 'in blind' mean on a binding?
answers: Decoration or lettering impressed into the covering material without gold or colour
Tooling applied to the inner surface of the boards
Decoration that has faded until barely visible
Tooling carried out without a design die, freehand
correct: Decoration or lettering impressed into the covering material without gold or colour

question: What is 'calf' in bookbinding?
answers: Smooth leather made from calfskin, the commonest leather used in bookbinding
A type of vellum made from young cattle
A coarse grain leather used for large folios
A paste-board used as an alternative to wooden boards
correct: Smooth leather made from calfskin, the commonest leather used in bookbinding

question: What is a 'colophon' in a book?
answers: An inscription at the end of a book giving details of its production such as printer, place and date
A decorative floral ornament used by a printer as a device
The first leaf of the first gathering
A cancelled title page retained at the end of the volume
correct: An inscription at the end of a book giving details of its production such as printer, place and date

question: What does 'ex libris' mean on a book?
answers: It is a bookplate or label identifying the owner, meaning 'from the books of'
It means the book has been removed from a library
It is a Latin phrase meaning the text is authentic
It describes a book printed without a named publisher
correct: It is a bookplate or label identifying the owner, meaning 'from the books of'

question: What are 'errata' in a book?
answers: Mistakes or misprints discovered after printing, listed for correction
Blank leaves deliberately included by the printer
Passages removed by censorship before publication
Printer's marks used to identify the edition
correct: Mistakes or misprints discovered after printing, listed for correction

question: What is an 'incunabulum' (plural: incunabula)?
answers: A book printed in Europe before 1501, in the cradle period of printing
A manuscript book decorated with gold initials
Any book printed before the introduction of the hand press
A block book printed entirely from woodcuts
correct: A book printed in Europe before 1501, in the cradle period of printing

question: What is the 'STC'?
answers: A catalogue of English books printed between 1475 and 1640, compiled by Pollard & Redgrave
The Society for the Trade of Cataloguers
A short-title catalogue of scientific and technical works
A catalogue of all books printed in the Stationers' Company register
correct: A catalogue of English books printed between 1475 and 1640, compiled by Pollard & Redgrave

question: What is 'Wing' in bibliographical reference?
answers: A short-title catalogue of English books printed between 1641 and 1700
A bibliography of English literature compiled by Donald Wing
A catalogue of books with engraved title pages
A record of books sold at the Wing auction house
correct: A short-title catalogue of English books printed between 1641 and 1700

question: What does 'ESTC' stand for in its current usage?
answers: English Short-Title Catalogue, an online catalogue covering STC, Wing and 18th-century books
Eighteenth-century Scientific and Technical Catalogue
European Short-Title Catalogue
English Subscription Title Catalogue
correct: English Short-Title Catalogue, an online catalogue covering STC, Wing and 18th-century books

question: What is 'BAL' in book collecting?
answers: Bibliography of American Literature, a detailed bibliography of principal American literary figures
Book Auction Listings, a record of American saleroom prices
British Antiquarian Library, a catalogue of rare British books
Bibliographical Annals of Literature, a general reference series
correct: Bibliography of American Literature, a detailed bibliography of principal American literary figures

question: What are 'deckle edges' on a book?
answers: The rough, untrimmed edges of hand-made paper, evidence that leaves are uncut
The bevelled edges of thick boards used in fine bindings
Edges gilded with a fine dotted or speckled pattern
Edges deliberately frayed to simulate age
correct: The rough, untrimmed edges of hand-made paper, evidence that leaves are uncut

question: What is 'foxing' in a book?
answers: Brown spotty staining on the pages caused by oxidisation or fungal growth
Rubbing of the gilt on leather spine panels
Fading of ink caused by exposure to light
Small holes made by bookworms in the text block
correct: Brown spotty staining on the pages caused by oxidisation or fungal growth

question: What is a 'half-title'?
answers: A leaf before the main title page bearing only the short title of the book
A title page that lists only the author's surname
A cancel title replacing a withdrawn edition
A printed paper label on the spine of a boards binding
correct: A leaf before the main title page bearing only the short title of the book

question: What does 'original boards' describe?
answers: The temporary paper-covered board binding in which many books were issued between about 1780 and the 1830s
Any book in its original cloth binding
Wooden boards used in bindings before the 16th century
Boards covered with the original publisher's decorative paper
correct: The temporary paper-covered board binding in which many books were issued between about 1780 and the 1830s

question: What is an 'endpaper'?
answers: The double leaves added at front and back by the binder, one leaf pasted to the board and one forming the first or last page of the volume
Any blank leaf printed at the end of the final gathering
The publisher's printed advertisements inserted at the back of the book
The protective tissue paper laid over an engraved plate
correct: The double leaves added at front and back by the binder, one leaf pasted to the board and one forming the first or last page of the volume

question: What is 'morocco' as a binding leather?
answers: Leather made from goatskin, originally associated with Morocco and the Levant, noted for its grain and durability
Leather made from calf hide treated with a Moroccan tanning process
A type of sheepskin dyed to resemble goatskin
A generic term for any decorative leather from North Africa
correct: Leather made from goatskin, originally associated with Morocco and the Levant, noted for its grain and durability

question: What is a 'dust jacket' (or dust wrapper)?
answers: The printed paper cover wrapped around a cased book to protect it
A glassine or tissue wrapper used by collectors to preserve fragile bindings
A plain paper cover used on advance review copies
The stiff cardboard slipcase that houses a fine binding
correct: The printed paper cover wrapped around a cased book to protect it

question: What does 'editio princeps' mean?
answers: The first printed edition of a work, especially a classical text
The most authoritative or definitive edition of a work
The first authorised edition, as opposed to a pirated one
The prince's personal copy of a royal presentation edition
correct: The first printed edition of a work, especially a classical text

question: What is a 'broadside' or 'broadsheet' in bibliographical terms?
answers: A piece of printed matter printed on one side only of a single undivided sheet
Any large-format newspaper
A pamphlet of four or more leaves
A folded sheet forming a single gathering
correct: A piece of printed matter printed on one side only of a single undivided sheet

question: In auction terminology, what does it mean when a lot is 'bought in'?
answers: The lot failed to reach its reserve price and was not sold
The auctioneer purchased the lot for the house's own stock
A trade buyer purchased the lot on behalf of a collector
The lot was withdrawn before the sale began
correct: The lot failed to reach its reserve price and was not sold

question: Who is the 'under-bidder' at an auction?
answers: The last unsuccessful bidder on a lot; the runner-up
A bidder who intentionally keeps prices low on behalf of a ring
A dealer bidding below the reserve to test the market
An agent bidding on commission without having viewed the lot
correct: The last unsuccessful bidder on a lot; the runner-up

question: What is a 'coloured copy' of an illustrated book?
answers: A copy in which the illustrations have been hand-coloured, either at the time of publication or later
A copy bound in a colour specified by the publisher as the primary binding
A copy with the text printed in more than one colour
A copy with the edges stained or sprinkled in colour
correct: A copy in which the illustrations have been hand-coloured, either at the time of publication or later

question: What does 'uncut' mean in a catalogue description?
answers: The edges of the leaves have not been trimmed by the binder's knife, preserving the original deckle
The book has never been opened or read
The signatures have not been separated from the original printed sheets
The volume retains its original sewing without rebinding
correct: The edges of the leaves have not been trimmed by the binder's knife, preserving the original deckle

question: What is the 'Grolier List'?
answers: One Hundred Books Famous in English Literature, a list published by the Grolier Club of New York in 1902
A checklist of fine bindings executed for Jean Grolier in the 16th century
An annual catalogue of the most valuable books sold at American auctions
A record of the books in the collection of the Grolier Club library
correct: One Hundred Books Famous in English Literature, a list published by the Grolier Club of New York in 1902

question: What are 'blockbooks' (xylographica)?
answers: Early European books in which both text and illustrations were cut on wood blocks and printed from them
Books whose covers are decorated with large metal block stamps
Books printed from metal type but illustrated exclusively with woodcuts
Manuscripts copied in a regular block script before the invention of printing
correct: Early European books in which both text and illustrations were cut on wood blocks and printed from them

question: When did the heyday of European blockbooks occur, according to modern research?
answers: The 1460s, not before the invention of movable type as once supposed
The 1420s, pre-dating Gutenberg's press
The 1480s, after movable type was already widespread
The 1500s, as a continuation of earlier manuscript traditions
correct: The 1460s, not before the invention of movable type as once supposed

question: What is 'bibliophily'?
answers: The love of books; a bibliophile is a lover of books
The systematic description of books as physical objects
The collecting of books as financial investments
The study of the history of printing and publishing
correct: The love of books; a bibliophile is a lover of books

question: What is 'bibliography' in the collector's sense?
answers: The study of books as physical objects and the systematic description of them
A reading list or guide to further study appended to a scholarly work
A catalogue of the works of a single author
The science of dating paper and ink to authenticate old books
correct: The study of books as physical objects and the systematic description of them

question: What is 'bisquing' in the context of old books?
answers: Obliterating passages by painting them out with black ink or overprinting with a blank block, usually for censorship
Applying a biscuit-coloured varnish to restore faded leather
Pressing the covers of a book in a heated press to restore them
Trimming the edges of a book with a bisecting knife
correct: Obliterating passages by painting them out with black ink or overprinting with a blank block, usually for censorship

question: What is a 'binding copy'?
answers: A copy whose covers are in poor condition but whose interior is clean and worth rebinding
A binder's reference copy kept to record a binding style
A copy in its original, unrestored publisher's binding
A copy specially bound for presentation by the author
correct: A copy whose covers are in poor condition but whose interior is clean and worth rebinding

question: What is the 'Cottage style' of binding?
answers: A style where the top and bottom of the spine panel slope away from a broken centre, creating a gabled effect
A plain, undecorated binding typical of country booksellers in the 17th century
A style using natural, undyed leather with flower and foliage tooling
A half-bound style with marbled boards popular in rural England
correct: A style where the top and bottom of the spine panel slope away from a broken centre, creating a gabled effect

question: What is the 'Etruscan style' of binding?
answers: Decoration of calf bindings by acid staining with classical ornament such as Greek vases and key patterns, popular c.1785–1820
A binding style imitating ancient Etruscan manuscripts, with ivory boards and metal clasps
A style associated with Italian Renaissance binders using interlace tooling
A Florentine binding technique using coloured inlays on vellum
correct: Decoration of calf bindings by acid staining with classical ornament such as Greek vases and key patterns, popular c.1785–1820

question: What does 'cropped' mean in a book description?
answers: The binder's knife has cut into the printed matter itself, not just the margins
The text block has been trimmed only very lightly, preserving most of the margin
The edges have been cut and coloured by the binder
The boards have been cut down shorter than the text block
correct: The binder's knife has cut into the printed matter itself, not just the margins

question: What is 'de-acidification' as applied to books?
answers: A process that removes acid from machine-made paper to slow its decay
A washing process to remove foxing stains from leaves
A chemical treatment to neutralise the residues of old leather dressings
The removal of acidic adhesives used in rebinding
correct: A process that removes acid from machine-made paper to slow its decay

question: What are 'copyright libraries' in the UK?
answers: Libraries legally entitled to receive a free copy of every book published in the UK, including the British Library, Bodleian, and Cambridge University Library
Libraries that hold the original copyright documents for important literary works
Libraries operated by the Publishers' Association to safeguard intellectual property
Libraries that specialise in books currently in copyright, as opposed to out-of-print works
correct: Libraries legally entitled to receive a free copy of every book published in the UK, including the British Library, Bodleian, and Cambridge University Library

question: What is a 'book-plate' (ex libris)?
answers: A label of ownership, usually affixed to a front endpaper, identifying the book's owner
A decorative plate frontispiece engraved for a specific owner's library
A printed broadside advertising the publication of a new book
A metal plate used for printing the book's illustrations
correct: A label of ownership, usually affixed to a front endpaper, identifying the book's owner

question: What is 'vellum' in bookbinding?
answers: A fine writing or binding material prepared from the stretched and dried skin of a calf, goat or sheep
A high-quality wove paper with a smooth, creamy finish
A type of imitation leather made from compressed cotton fibres
A translucent protective paper used as a fly-leaf in fine bindings
correct: A fine writing or binding material prepared from the stretched and dried skin of a calf, goat or sheep

question: What does 'Aldine' refer to in book collecting?
answers: Books printed by Aldus Manutius in Venice, or things associated with him, including his dolphin-and-anchor device
Books printed in the Aldine type designed by William Pickering
The pocket-sized format introduced by Aldus Manutius, irrespective of the printer
Any book printed in italic type, which Aldus invented
correct: Books printed by Aldus Manutius in Venice, or things associated with him, including his dolphin-and-anchor device

question: What is a 'provenance' in book collecting?
answers: The history of a book's ownership, traced through inscriptions, bookplates, sale records and other evidence
The place and date of a book's printing as stated in the imprint
The list of sources cited by the author in the text
A bookseller's written guarantee of a book's authenticity
correct: The history of a book's ownership, traced through inscriptions, bookplates, sale records and other evidence

question: What is a 'gutter' in a book?
answers: To a bookseller, the inner margin between two facing pages; to a printer, the space between fore-edges of two abutting pages in the forme
The groove pressed into the spine of a cased book at the hinge
The channel cut by the plough along the edges of the text block
The space between the boards and the text block at the head and tail
correct: To a bookseller, the inner margin between two facing pages; to a printer, the space between fore-edges of two abutting pages in the forme

question: What is a 'blurb' in cataloguing terms?
answers: A persuasive or laudatory note added by a bookseller to a catalogue description, as distinct from objective bibliographical description
The publisher's promotional text on the dust jacket
A brief note describing the physical condition of a book
An annotation in the author's hand found in a copy
correct: A persuasive or laudatory note added by a bookseller to a catalogue description, as distinct from objective bibliographical description

question: What does 'called for' mean in a bookseller's catalogue?
answers: Required to be present according to a named bibliography or authority, e.g. 'with the errata slip called for by Jones'
A book actively sought by a named collector
A volume requested from a library for consultation
An item reserved for a specific buyer before the catalogue is issued
correct: Required to be present according to a named bibliography or authority, e.g. 'with the errata slip called for by Jones'

question: What are 'guide letters' in early printed books?
answers: Small letters printed in the space left for a hand-painted initial, to guide the illuminator as to which letter was required
The running headings printed at the top of each page to guide the reader
Letters printed in the margin to guide the binder in gathering the quires
Italic letters used to distinguish proper names in the text
correct: Small letters printed in the space left for a hand-painted initial, to guide the illuminator as to which letter was required

question: What is a 'gutta-percha binding'?
answers: A binding using a rubber solution instead of sewing to hold the leaves together, developed from about 1840
A binding using gutta-percha-treated cloth as a covering material
A binding technique using natural resin to consolidate fragile gatherings
A binding with a spine made from hardened vegetable rubber
correct: A binding using a rubber solution instead of sewing to hold the leaves together, developed from about 1840

question: What is 'breaking up' a book?
answers: Dismantling a book and selling its plates or leaves separately, regarded by collectors as vandalism
Repairing a book whose hinges and spine have broken down
Separating the gatherings of an unsewn book for conservation
Removing a book from a composite volume bound together by a previous owner
correct: Dismantling a book and selling its plates or leaves separately, regarded by collectors as vandalism

question: What is 'cannibalisation' in book collecting?
answers: Breaking up two or more copies of a book to assemble a single 'complete' copy containing variants never originally found together
Removing plates from an imperfect copy to complete another copy of the same book
Using leaves from a damaged copy to repair another copy of a different edition
Selling individual leaves of a rare book rather than the book as a whole
correct: Breaking up two or more copies of a book to assemble a single 'complete' copy containing variants never originally found together

question: What is a 'first edition in book form'?
answers: The first time a work previously published in a periodical, broadside or other format appeared between its own covers as a book
The first edition of a book issued in hardback, as distinct from a paperback
The first edition to be bound in publisher's cloth rather than boards
The first edition published in the author's home country
correct: The first time a work previously published in a periodical, broadside or other format appeared between its own covers as a book

question: What is 'de-accessioning'?
answers: The process by which a library disposes of books deemed surplus to requirements, frequently a euphemism for sale
The formal removal of a book from the copyright register
The cataloguing of newly acquired books into a library's collection
The transfer of a book from active shelves to archive storage
correct: The process by which a library disposes of books deemed surplus to requirements, frequently a euphemism for sale

question: What is an 'authorised edition' in book collecting, and why might a collector prefer the unauthorised one?
answers: The first edition published with the author's consent; collectors often also prize the earlier unauthorised printing for its chronological priority
The edition approved by the publisher as definitive; collectors prefer it for textual accuracy
An edition licensed by the author's estate after the author's death
The edition bearing the author's own annotations and corrections
correct: The first edition published with the author's consent; collectors often also prize the earlier unauthorised printing for its chronological priority

question: What is 'buckram'?
answers: A durable, firmly woven linen or cotton bookbinding cloth with a smooth calendered finish
A coarse canvas used only for library rebindings
A stiffened paper used as a substitute for boards in cheap bindings
A type of straw-board used as the base for the sides of a bound book
correct: A durable, firmly woven linen or cotton bookbinding cloth with a smooth calendered finish

question: What is 'American Book Prices Current' (ABPC)?
answers: An annual record of books, manuscripts and autographs sold at auction in America and the principal London and European houses, published since 1895
A quarterly catalogue of prices charged by American antiquarian booksellers
A database of list prices for new books published in the United States
A record of American auction results for books only, excluding manuscripts
correct: An annual record of books, manuscripts and autographs sold at auction in America and the principal London and European houses, published since 1895

question: What is the 'Cambridge style' of binding?
answers: A calf binding style with a rectangular frame left plain and the central rectangle mottled or sprinkled, popular with Cambridge binders in the early 18th century
A style of binding associated with the Cambridge University Press using dark blue cloth
A half-calf style using a distinctive Cambridge tan leather
A vellum binding style developed at Cambridge for presentation copies
correct: A calf binding style with a rectangular frame left plain and the central rectangle mottled or sprinkled, popular with Cambridge binders in the early 18th century

question: What is 'gothicc type' (black letter) in early books?
answers: A family of typefaces that do not look like roman or italic, including textura, rotunda and bastarda, used widely in Europe before roman type became dominant
Any typeface printed in black ink at a large display size
A heavy typeface used exclusively for Bible printing throughout the hand-press era
The typeface invented by Gutenberg and used only in Germany
correct: A family of typefaces that do not look like roman or italic, including textura, rotunda and bastarda, used widely in Europe before roman type became dominant

question: What does 'as issued' signify in a catalogue description?
answers: The feature described is the original condition as the publisher issued it, even if it contradicts normal expectation
The book is in the same condition as when the cataloguer received it
The book has not been rebound or restored since purchase
The book is accompanied by the original publisher's invoice
correct: The feature described is the original condition as the publisher issued it, even if it contradicts normal expectation

question: What is 'encapsulation' as a conservation technique?
answers: Vacuum-sealing a single sheet within an envelope of inert plastic to protect it from decay and handling
Coating a fragile binding in a protective layer of consolidant
Storing a book in a sealed acid-free box to prevent atmospheric damage
Laminating a damaged leaf between sheets of Japanese tissue paper
correct: Vacuum-sealing a single sheet within an envelope of inert plastic to protect it from decay and handling
